title: Wireless Access Points and Controllers
description: Wi-Fi fundamentals, SSIDs, security standards, and enterprise wireless management.

slides.title: The Wireless Access Point (WAP) 📡
slides.content:
# Bridging the Air Gap

A **Wireless Access Point (AP)** connects wireless devices (clients) to a **wired network**.

**Analogy:** The AP is like a **Translator** at the United Nations.
- Speaks **802.11 (Wi-Fi)** on one side (radio waves).
- Speaks **802.3 (Ethernet)** on the other side (copper cable).

**How it Works:**
- The AP broadcasts a **Beacon Frame** every ~100ms advertising its **SSID** (Network Name).
- Clients hear the beacon and send an **Association Request**.
- The AP authenticates the client (Open, WPA2-Personal, WPA2-Enterprise).
- Once associated, the AP acts as a **Layer 2 Bridge**. It forwards frames between the wireless client and the wired switch.

**Important:** A **Home Router** is actually **three devices in one box**:
1.  Router (Layer 3)
2.  Switch (Layer 2)
3.  Access Point (Wireless Bridge)

In enterprise networks, these functions are **separate hardware** for scalability and performance.

quizData.question: What is the primary function of an Access Point?

options.text: Bridge wireless clients to a wired network.

quizData.explanation: APs are Layer 2 bridges connecting 802.11 Wi-Fi to 802.3 Ethernet.

slides.title: Wi-Fi Frequencies and Standards 📶
slides.content:
# 2.4 GHz vs. 5 GHz vs. 6 GHz

| Band | Channels | Speed | Range | Interference |
|------|----------|-------|-------|--------------|
| **2.4 GHz** | 11 (Only 3 non-overlapping) | Slower | **Longer** (penetrates walls) | **High** (Microwaves, Bluetooth, neighbors) |
| **5 GHz** | Many (DFS) | Faster | Shorter (weaker through walls) | **Low** |
| **6 GHz (Wi-Fi 6E/7)** | Massive | Fastest | Shortest | **Very Low** |

**Wi-Fi Generations:**
- **Wi-Fi 4 (802.11n):** MIMO introduced.
- **Wi-Fi 5 (802.11ac):** 5 GHz only, MU-MIMO downlink.
- **Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax):** OFDMA (efficiency in crowded spaces), 2.4 & 5 GHz.
- **Wi-Fi 7 (802.11be):** 320 MHz channels, insane speeds.

**Cybersecurity Note:** **2.4 GHz is a nightmare for security.** Because it penetrates walls so well, your office Wi-Fi leaks into the parking lot. Attackers can sit in a car and **sniff traffic** or attempt **Deauthentication Attacks** from outside the building.

slides.title: Wireless Security: WEP, WPA, WPA2, WPA3 🔐
slides.content:
# The Evolution of Wi-Fi Encryption

**NEVER use these (Broken):**
- **WEP (Wired Equivalent Privacy):** Cracked in minutes. Uses weak RC4 encryption.
- **WPA (Wi-Fi Protected Access):** Uses TKIP. Also deprecated.

**Acceptable (Home Use):**
- **WPA2-Personal (PSK):** Uses **AES** encryption with a **Pre-Shared Key (Password)**.
- **Weakness:** Everyone uses the same password. If an attacker captures the **4-Way Handshake**, they can run a **dictionary attack** offline to crack the password.

**Best (Enterprise Use):**
- **WPA2-Enterprise / WPA3-Enterprise:** Uses **802.1X** authentication.
- Each user has a **unique username and password** verified against a **RADIUS server** (Active Directory).
- **Advantage:** If Bob leaves the company, you disable his account. You don't have to change the Wi-Fi password for everyone.

**WPA3 Improvements:**
- **SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals):** Replaces PSK with a secure handshake that is resistant to offline dictionary attacks (Dragonfly).
- **Forward Secrecy:** Even if the password is cracked later, past traffic cannot be decrypted.

slides.title: Deauthentication Attack 🥷
slides.content:
## Kicking Users Off Wi-Fi

A **Deauthentication Attack** exploits a flaw in the 802.11 management frames.

- Management frames are **NOT encrypted** (even on WPA2).
- An attacker can **spoof** a Deauth frame that appears to come from the AP.
- The client is forced to disconnect and **reconnect**.
- The attacker captures the **new 4-Way Handshake** and cracks the password offline.

**Defense:** **802.11w (Protected Management Frames)** . This encrypts management frames, preventing spoofed deauth packets. Enabled by default in WPA3.

slides.title: Wireless Controllers and CAPWAP 🕹️
slides.content:
# Managing Many APs

In an office with 50 APs, logging into each one individually is impossible.

**Solution: Wireless LAN Controller (WLC)**

- The WLC is a central brain that manages all **Lightweight Access Points (LWAPs)**.
- APs find the WLC automatically and download their configuration.
- The WLC handles **Roaming** (moving from AP1 to AP2 without dropping the call).
- The WLC enforces **RF Management** (adjusting power levels to reduce interference).

**Protocol:** **CAPWAP (Control and Provisioning of Wireless Access Points)**
- **Control Channel:** Encrypted DTLS tunnel for configuration. (Like SSH for APs).
- **Data Channel:** Optional tunnel for client traffic (can be split at the AP or centralized at the WLC).

**Analogy:** The WLC is the **Conductor** of an orchestra. The APs are the musicians. Without the conductor, you just have noise. With the conductor, you have beautiful, coordinated Wi-Fi coverage.

quizData.question: What is the primary benefit of a Wireless LAN Controller (WLC) in an enterprise?

options.text: It centralizes management and configuration of multiple APs.
It replaces the need for switches.

quizData.explanation: The WLC provides a single pane of glass for managing hundreds of APs, including RF settings and security policies.
